opts: Shift up and toward the puck as a unit to pressure and close lanes
Collapse tight to the crease and concede the point shot rather than pressure
Send the strong-side forward alone to pressure the point and hold the box
Hold your box spacing until the puck crosses the blue line
Tell your linemates what you're seeing so they adjust on the next go
Keep it to yourself since the coaches are the ones making the adjustments
Yell at the on-ice players to fix the coverage before the next whistle
Wait until after the game to bring it up in the locker room
Stay slightly behind and to the outside to be an outlet if they get pressured or need support
Skate ahead of the play into the zone to get open for a stretch pass even if it risks going offside
Stay at the exact same height as them since parallel support is the strongest outlet option
Slow down and drift back toward the defensive zone to cover in case there is a turnover
Tape-to-tape — lead them slightly so the puck arrives in stride without them slowing down at all
Right at their feet so they have to stop skating and collect it before continuing up the wall
Behind them so they have to turn back for it and reset their route through the neutral zone
Loft it high over the forechecker so the puck drops into open space near your winger further up the ice
Use D-to-D movement and east-west passes to pull the trap apart before trying to carry through it
Keep carrying through the neutral zone since speed and skill will eventually break any trap formation
Dump the puck deep on every entry and forget about trying to carry through the trap at center
Have your most skilled player try to beat the trap one-on-four since nobody else can handle it
Increase offensive zone time through sustained pressure and cycling — force turnovers in their zone to create chances rather than rushing the attack up the ice
Pull the goalie at the start of the third to get an extra attacker for the full twenty minutes since you need a goal and pulling early maximizes chances
Keep playing the same system and wait for a lucky bounce since changing approach when trailing usually makes things worse and creates more turnovers against structure
Play ultra-aggressive with no regard for defense since you need a goal and the best defense when trailing is a strong offense that keeps constant pressure on their zone
Switch to a different technique — use a backhand tie-up or a draw to a different spot. Varying your faceoff technique prevents the opponent from timing your move consistently.
Keep using the same forehand technique since it has worked all game and a single adjustment from the opposing center shouldn't make you change an approach that keeps winning draws
Stop chasing faceoff wins and focus on tying up the opposing center so the wingers can battle for the puck since who wins the draw matters less than who wins the board battle
Let the opposing center win every draw for the rest of the game since they have figured out your technique and no mid-game adjustment will be effective against a fully adjusted opponent
